:::{#def-ph-interval-fail-prob}
#### Interval failure probability

Fix an interval width $\Delta t$ and grid points $\tau$.
For subject $j$, the **interval failure probability** at $\tau$ is the
conditional probability that $j$ fails during $[\tau, \tau + \Delta t)$,
given that $j$ is still at risk at $\tau$:

$$
q_j(\tau) \eqdef \Pr\sb{j \text{ fails in } [\tau,\, \tau + \Delta t) \mid j \text{ at risk at } \tau}.
$$

To first order in $\Delta t$, this probability is the hazard times the interval width.
We give that approximation its own symbol, the **first-order interval failure probability**,
and split it into baseline and covariate factors via the proportional-hazards decomposition (@thm-ph-haz-decomp):

$$
q^*_j(\tau) \eqdef \haz(\tau \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t = \dblue{\haz_0(\tau)\,\Delta t}\;\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)},
$$

so that

$$
q_j(\tau) \approx q^*_j(\tau).
$$
:::

:::{#exm-ph-interval-fail-prob}
#### Interval failure probability: exact value versus first-order approximation

Suppose subject $j$ has constant hazard $\haz(t \mid \vx_j) = 0.1$ per year,
and consider the half-year interval $[4.0,\, 4.5)$, so $\Delta t = 0.5$ years.

**Exact interval failure probability.**
Using the [survival/cumulative-hazard relationship](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#cor-surv-int-haz),
the exact probability that $j$ fails in this interval, given survival to year $4$, is

$$
\ba
q_j(4.0)
&= \Pr\sb{j \text{ fails in } [4.0,\, 4.5) \mid j \text{ at risk at } 4.0}
&& \text{(definition of } q_j \text{)}
\\
&= 1 - \Pr\sb{j \text{ survives past } 4.5 \mid j \text{ at risk at } 4.0}
&& \text{(complement rule)}
\\
&= 1 - \frac{\surv(4.5 \mid \vx_j)}{\surv(4.0 \mid \vx_j)}
&& \text{(conditional survival as a ratio)}
\\
&= 1 - \frac{\expf{-\int_{0}^{4.5} \haz(u \mid \vx_j)\,du}}
            {\expf{-\int_{0}^{4.0} \haz(u \mid \vx_j)\,du}}
&& \text{(survival from cumulative hazard)}
\\
&= 1 - \expf{-\int_{4.0}^{4.5} \haz(u \mid \vx_j)\,du}
&& \text{(quotient of exponentials)}
\\
&= 1 - \expf{-\int_{4.0}^{4.5} 0.1 \,du}
&& \text{(substitute } \haz(u \mid \vx_j) = 0.1\text{)}
\\
&= 1 - \expf{-0.1 \times 0.5}
&& \text{(constant integrand over width } \Delta t = 0.5\text{)}
\\
&= 1 - \expf{-0.05}
&& \text{(arithmetic)}
\\
&\approx 0.0488 .
&& \text{(evaluate } e^{-0.05} \approx 0.9512\text{)}
\ea
$$

**First-order approximation.**
The first-order approximation replaces the exact value with the hazard times the interval width:

$$
\ba
q^*_j(4.0)
&= \haz(4.0 \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t
&& \text{(definition of } q^*_j \text{)}
\\
&= 0.1 \times 0.5
&& \text{(substitute } \haz = 0.1,\ \Delta t = 0.5\text{)}
\\
&= 0.05 .
&& \text{(arithmetic)}
\ea
$$

**Contrast.**
The first-order approximation $q^*_j(4.0) = 0.05$ overstates the exact value $q_j(4.0) \approx 0.0488$
by about $0.0012$, a relative error of roughly $2.5\%$.
The discrepancy is exactly the leading term dropped by the linearization
$1 - \expf{-x} = x - \frac{x^2}{2} + \cdots$:
here $x = \haz(4.0 \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t = 0.05$,
so the dropped term is $\frac{x^2}{2} = \frac{0.05^2}{2} \approx 0.00125$,
which shrinks to zero as $\Delta t \to 0$.
This vanishing discrepancy is why the proof's discretization becomes exact in the continuous-time limit.
:::
